figures.gloss: The Storm-God 'the Conqueror', bull-drawn lord of thunder and rain who rose to head the pantheon by smiting the serpent Illuyanka — the king of the gods of the Hatti, syncretised with Hurrian Teshub.
figures.facet: The shard where the storm-god's kingship is shown to be EARNED and even contingent: Tarhunna can lose his heart to the dragon and must be restored by trick and sacrifice — divine sovereignty as a thing that can be stolen back.
figures.provenance: Hittite state cult; Hurrian Teshub absorbed via syncretism. Tarhunna < PIE *(s)terh2- 'to conquer/overcome', and the storm-god type < the Indo-European *Perkwunos complex.

figures.gloss: The great serpent who defeated the Storm-God in their first encounter, taking his heart and eyes — overcome only by the goddess Inara's feast-trap or by a mortal's ruse, in a myth recited at the spring purulli festival.
figures.facet: The dragon who WINS the first round: where most Chaoskampf opens with the hero ascendant, the Anatolian serpent first humiliates the god, making the slaying a recovery of stolen order rather than a simple triumph.
figures.provenance: Two versions in CTH 321, the purulli-festival text. Cited as a Near-Eastern source-type for the Greek Typhon battle.

figures.gloss: The vanishing god of agriculture and fertility, who in a rage stalks off and hides in the moor, so that hearth-fires die, beasts and grain go barren — and must be hunted, soothed and led home before life resumes.
figures.facet: The dying-and-rising god who never DIES: he merely withdraws in anger and is fetched back, so the cycle of sterility-and-return is dramatised as a divine sulk and reconciliation, not a death in the underworld.
figures.provenance: The Telipinu Myth, CTH 324; a 'disappearing god' (mugawar) ritual text.

figures.gloss: The 'father of the gods' of the Hurrian-Hittite succession, who bit off and swallowed the genitals of the sky-god Anu, was made pregnant with the storm-god thereby, and schemed his ousted dynasty's revenge by spawning the stone giant Ullikummi.
figures.facet: The deposed elder god as eternal PLOTTER: Kumarbi loses the throne yet never accepts it, breeding monster after monster against the new order — the succession myth read from the loser's side.
figures.provenance: The 'Kingship in Heaven' / Song of Kumarbi cycle, CTH 344, Hurrian origin. The ATTESTED source-type behind Hesiod's Ouranos–Kronos–Zeus succession.

figures.gloss: The Sky, primordial king of heaven in the Kumarbi cycle, who held the kingship for nine ages before Kumarbi unmanned and overthrew him — the borrowed Mesopotamian An standing at the head of the Anatolian succession.
figures.facet: The sky-father as the FIRST to fall: here the heaven-king is not the eternal ruler but merely the opening link in a chain of violent successions, the deposed sky whose severed seed still fathers the god who will dethrone his usurper.
figures.provenance: Song of Kumarbi (CTH 344); the figure is the Mesopotamian An/Anu adopted into the Hurrian-Hittite myth. A documented borrowing, not a cognate.

figures.gloss: Wurusemu, the Sun-Goddess of the city Arinna — patron of the Hittite king and queen, mistress of judgement and the land, the foremost deity of the realm alongside her consort the Storm-God.
figures.facet: The shard where the sun is sovereign QUEEN and mother of the state, not a male eye in the sky: solar majesty fused with maternal patronage of kingship, the radiant judge who legitimises the throne.
figures.provenance: Prayers of Hittite kings and queens (e.g. the prayer of Puduhepa). Anatolia keeps a female sun, like Baltic Saulė and Japanese Amaterasu.

figures.gloss: Daughter of the Storm-God, goddess of the wild beasts who devises the banquet-trap that makes the serpent Illuyanka drunk and bound, recruiting the mortal Hupasiya to bind the snake so her father can kill him.
figures.facet: The dragon-slaying delegated to a daughter's CUNNING: it is not the god's raw strength but a goddess's stratagem and a mortal ally that undo the serpent — order restored by trickery and a human bargain.
figures.provenance: Illuyanka myth, version 1 (CTH 321). Her name is plausibly related to that of Inanna/Ishtar but the connection is debated.

figures.gloss: The 'Grandmother', mother-goddess of birth and fate, whose bee she sends out to find the vanished Telipinu and sting him awake — the matriarchal power who knows where lost divinity has gone.
figures.facet: The great mother as FINDER of the lost god: her wisdom and her bee are what locate and rouse the withdrawn deity, the maternal principle as the agent of cosmic recovery.
figures.provenance: Telipinu Myth and related disappearing-god texts; her name is the reduplicated Hattic/Hittite 'hanna', grandmother.

figures.gloss: The great spring New-Year festival of renewal at which the Illuyanka myth was recited, re-enacting the Storm-God's victory so that 'the land may prosper and thrive' for another agricultural year.
figures.facet: Where myth is not told but PERFORMED to make the year turn: the serpent-slaying is a calendrical engine, the recitation itself the ritual machinery that re-establishes fertile order each spring.
figures.provenance: Framing rubric of CTH 321; 'purulli' likely from Hattic 'puru', earth/ground — a festival of and for the land.

figures.gloss: The blind, deaf stone giant Kumarbi sires upon a rock to grow up from the sea and topple the Storm-God's reign — severed at last from his base on Upelluri's shoulder by the primeval cutting-tool that once split heaven from earth.
figures.facet: The adversary who is pure unfeeling MATTER: a diorite colossus with no senses and no will of its own, the rebellion of inert stone against living order, defeated by recalling the world's first act of separation.
figures.provenance: The Song of Ullikummi, CTH 345, Hurrian-Hittite Kumarbi cycle.

figures.gloss: Goddess of magic, medicine and incantation who performs the ritual that pacifies Telipinu's rage in the myth's climax — mixing honey, oil and other substances in a soothing rite explicitly framed as the divine model for the human incantation-priest's own healing magic.
figures.facet: Kamrušepa supplies the myth's actual mechanism of resolution: Hannahanna's bee only locates the vanished god, it is Kamrušepa's ritual craft that calms him — magic itself deified as the missing link between search and reconciliation.
figures.provenance: Telipinu Myth, CTH 324; Billie Jean Collins (ed.), 'The Context of Scripture' I; Volkert Haas, 'Geschichte der hethitischen Religion' (1994).

figures.gloss: Ištanu (Hattic Eštan lineage, equated with Hurrian Šimige), the male Sun-God of Heaven and celestial judge of gods and men, invoked first in Hittite royal prayers such as the prayer of Kantuzzili — distinct from, and complementary to, the female Sun-Goddess of Arinna.
figures.facet: The Hittite pantheon keeps two suns rather than one: Ištanu the impartial celestial judge addressed in private, personal prayer, and Arinna the state's civic solar queen — sovereignty and justice split across a masculine sky-sun and a feminine city-sun.
figures.provenance: Prayer of Kantuzzili (CTH 373); royal invocation lists (e.g. Prayer of Muwatalli II); Volkert Haas, 'Geschichte der hethitischen Religion'; Gary Beckman (trans.), 'Hittite Prayers' (2nd ed., SBL, 2011).

figures.gloss: 'The calf of Teshub' — mountain-god son of the Storm-God and (via the Hurrian Hebat/Sun-Goddess of Arinna equation) the chief goddess, tutelary deity of the late Hittite Empire kings. He is carved at Yazılıkaya embracing King Tudḫaliya IV in a gesture of personal divine protection.
figures.facet: Šarruma's cult marks the empire's late turn toward personal, individualized royal patron-gods rather than only the state's collective pantheon — kingship guaranteed by a private divine son alongside the storm-god.
figures.provenance: Yazılıkaya reliefs (Chamber A and B); Volkert Haas, 'Geschichte der hethitischen Religion'; Piotr Taracha, 'Religions of Second Millennium Anatolia' (2009).

figures.gloss: Sovereign deity of the dark earth and the dead — originally a Hattic male god later increasingly treated as a goddess in Hittite state cult — petitioned above all in royal substitution rituals to accept a substitute victim and spare the king's life.
figures.facet: Lelwani supplies the pantheon's chthonic ruler otherwise missing from its storm-and-sun-dominated public face: not a punisher but a sovereign to be bargained with, receiving the substitute so the living king may be released.
figures.provenance: Ritual of the Royal Substitute (CTH 419-421); Volkert Haas, 'Geschichte der hethitischen Religion'; Piotr Taracha, 'Religions of Second Millennium Anatolia'.

figures.gloss: The mortal man of Zigaratta recruited by the goddess Inara as her lover and ally in version 1 of the Illuyanka myth (CTH 321): he hides and binds the drunken, feasted serpent with a rope so the Storm-God can kill it, then lives with Inara until he breaks her taboo against looking out the window at his own family and is put to death for it.
figures.facet: The serpent-slaying's actual physical labor falls to a human hireling, not a god: Ḫupašiya is the myth's reminder that divine order in Hatti is restored through a mortal bargain and its cost, not through force alone.
figures.provenance: Illuyanka Myth, version 1, CTH 321; Harry A. Hoffner Jr., 'Hittite Myths' (2nd ed., 1998).

figures.gloss: The standard treaty-formula invocation — 'the Thousand Gods of Hatti' — listing storm-gods, sun-deities, mountains, rivers and springs of the whole empire (and, in international treaties, the other party's gods reciprocally) as sworn witnesses and enforcers of the oath.
figures.facet: The formula turns the pantheon into an instrument of statecraft: a treaty's binding power rests on assembling the empire's entire absorbed divine population as co-signatories, making Hittite religion's famous absorptive character a matter of documented diplomatic practice, not just myth.
figures.provenance: Treaty of Kadesh (Ḫattušili III / Ramesses II, CTH 91); Šuppiluliuma-Šattiwaza treaty (CTH 51); Gary Beckman, 'Hittite Diplomatic Texts' (2nd ed., 1999).

figures.gloss: The great 'crocus' spring festival of the Hittite state cult, in which the king and queen processed over roughly 38 days through the empire's sacred cities, renewing the cult images and offerings of the major gods for the coming year.
figures.facet: Where the purulli festival concentrates renewal into a single recited myth, the AN.TAḪ.ŠUM enacts it as logistics — an itinerant royal circuit binding the kingdom's scattered shrines into one annual, physically enacted confirmation of cosmic and political order.
figures.provenance: AN.TAḪ.ŠUM festival texts (CTH 604-624); Volkert Haas, 'Geschichte der hethitischen Religion'; Piotr Taracha, 'Religions of Second Millennium Anatolia'.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A colossal serpentine adversary that disrupts the natural celestial order and must be actively repelled by communal effort.
GOLD SEAM: Gods of agricultural renewal who vanish or withhold their life-giving essence, requiring intricate ritual appeasement to restore the world.
GOLD SEAM: Invincible, earth-born giants who are utterly impervious to physical defeat as long as they remain connected to their terrestrial base.
GOLD SEAM: The vanishing agricultural deities whose sudden disappearance beneath the earth paralyzes all fertility until they are pacified and returned.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient Mediterranean mother goddess who frantically searches for the missing god of fertility, forcing the restoration of life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both revolve around cyclical community feasts where humans and gods share the yield of the earth, reinforcing cosmic order through reciprocal hospitality.
The Anatolian Chaoskampf within the tradition: Storm-God against serpent, but a combat the god first LOSES — the slaying is a recovery of his stolen heart and eyes.
Father and daughter: the Storm-God's victory over Illuyanka is engineered by Inara's banquet-trap and her mortal ally — strength enabled by cunning.
The succession's hinge: Kumarbi unmans and dethrones the sky-king Anu, swallowing his seed and thereby becoming pregnant with his own future supplanter — usurpation that breeds its own undoing.
The storm-god is born FROM the body of his enemy Kumarbi (conceived by the swallowed seed of Anu) and must overthrow him to reign — the new order gestated inside the old.
Kumarbi engineers Ullikummi as his instrument of revenge — the deposed elder god spawning a stone champion against the reigning storm-god.
The Illuyanka myth is the recited heart of the purulli spring festival — the serpent's defeat re-performed to renew the agricultural year.
The mother-goddess Hannahanna sends her bee to find and sting awake the vanished Telipinu — the great mother as the agent who recovers the withdrawn god.
Within Anatolia, two faces of seasonal renewal: the disappearing-god ritual (Telipinu) and the New-Year serpent-slaying (purulli) both work to restart fertile order.
The Sun-Goddess of Arinna and the Storm-God form the chief divine couple at the head of the Hittite state pantheon, jointly patrons of the throne.

THE flagship transmission seam: the Hurrian-Hittite 'Kingship in Heaven' (Anu–Kumarbi–Storm-God) is the documented Near-Eastern source-type behind Hesiod's Ouranos–Kronos–Zeus succession, the castration-of-the-sky motif carried west.
The succession theme reaches Rome through the Greek inheritance — Jupiter as heir of the same dethroning-the-elder pattern Hesiod drew from the Kumarbi cycle.

The Storm-God-vs-serpent combat is widely held to underlie the Greek Zeus–Typhon battle (Typhon localised in Cilicia, on Anatolia's edge) — an attested Near-Eastern-to-Greek transmission of the Chaoskampf.

Both descend from the Indo-European thunder-deity complex (*Perkwunos / the dragon-slaying storm-wielder); Tarhunna's heart-and-eyes loss to Illuyanka rhymes with Thor's hammer-quest against the world-serpent.
Shared Indo-European storm-and-serpent inheritance: as Indra slays Vrtra to release the waters, Tarhunna slays Illuyanka to restore the land — the same Chaoskampf from a common reconstructed root.

Storm-gods of the same Near-Eastern milieu: Baal Hadad's combat with Yam/Lotan and Teshub's with Illuyanka share the West-Semitic/Anatolian storm-vs-serpent type, with documented cultural contact across the region.
Both are dragon-slaying storm-sovereigns of the Indo-European thunder-type; the seam is structural here, the firmer cognate running through the *Perkwunos reconstruction.
The world-serpent as the storm-god's appointed adversary: Illuyanka and Jormungandr each embody the chaos-snake the thunderer must face, though Anatolia's serpent first prevails.
Both dramatise seasonal sterility through a deity's absence — but Telipinu vanishes in WRATH and is fetched home, where Persephone is abducted below; withdrawal versus descent.
Vegetation-gods whose absence withers the land; Anatolia's distinctive twist is that Telipinu is not killed or taken to the underworld but simply hides in anger, then is reconciled.
Both are gods on whom the land's fertility hangs, lost and recovered — yet Osiris dies and is reassembled, while Telipinu only withdraws: the dying-rising joint without the death.
The divine withdrawal that blights the earth: Demeter's grieving retreat blights the grain as Telipinu's wrathful disappearance does — fertility held hostage to a god's mood.
Two FEMALE suns sovereign over their pantheons — the Sun-Goddess of Arinna and Amaterasu both invert the usual male solar eye; Anatolia's sun is queen and patron of kingship.
Solar deity as the legitimiser of sovereignty — Ra fathers the pharaoh's rule as Arinna's sun-goddess underwrites the Hittite king's; sun as throne-maker, here female.
The Anatolian Anu of the Kumarbi cycle is the Mesopotamian sky-god An/Anu adopted into Hurrian-Hittite myth — a documented borrowing of the heaven-king into the succession story.

Both belong to the Near-Eastern generational-combat myth-world (Enuma Elish's Tiamat-slaying, the Kumarbi cycle's succession), sharing the theme of a new divine order won by violence against an elder power.

The stone giant grown from the sea against the gods rhymes with Tiamat's brood of monsters in the Babylonian combat-myth — the elder power's monstrous champion confronted by the storm-champion of the new order.

A reach across families: both are deposed-yet-scheming powers driving the cosmos through cycles of overthrow — but no contact whatsoever; flagged speculative, a bare structural rhyme of the eternal usurper.

Ritual re-enactment as cosmic maintenance: the purulli recitation of the serpent's defeat and the Mesoamerican ballgame both stage a mythic combat to keep the world's order turning — independent inventions of the same logic.

A tempting but unproven link: Inara's name has been compared to Inanna/Ishtar, and both are fierce goddesses of the wild and of stratagem — but the etymological connection is contested, so flagged speculative.
GOLD SEAM: Fully synchronized in the Hittite empire, where Teshub was worshipped as the supreme storm-god of Hatti.
GOLD SEAM: The Hurrian father of the gods was adopted wholesale into Hittite state mythology.
GOLD SEAM: Hebat was formally equated with the Hittite Sun Goddess of Arinna, blending Syrian mother-goddess and Anatolian solar motifs.
GOLD SEAM: Alalu is the primal thesis overthrown by Anu's antithesis, initiating the violent cycle of cosmic succession.
GOLD SEAM: The Hurrian stone-giant was incorporated directly into the Hittite mythological canon.
GOLD SEAM: Monstrous sea and storm serpents engineered to threaten the divine order, though Hedammu is seduced rather than struck down.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive winter festival designed to forcefully renew the cosmos and defeat the stagnation of the dark half of the year.

Two storm-and-rain sky-powers whose godhead is bound up with combat — Tsui-//Goab warring eternally against //Gaunab as the Hittite weather-god battles the serpent Illuyanka. The supreme rain-bringer proven by his defeat-and-recovery in cosmic war.
GOLD SEAM: A primal, draconic representation of chaos that must be subdued by divine champions to allow the proper order to function.

Same chaoskampf furniture, opposite casting: the Hittite Illuyanka is the serpent the storm-god must DEFEAT, while Treng-Treng is the serpent who SAVES — the snake as foil in Anatolia, as rescuer in Araucanía; flagged as a reach on the shared serpent-combat frame.

Great communal renewal-festivals that re-secure the cosmic order: the Mapuche ngillatun for fertility and right relation with the supreme rhymes with the Hittite Purulli new-year rite — the world's order repaired by the gathered people, two unconnected liturgies of renewal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared structure of a vital annual festival that ritually vanquishes cosmic entropy and physically renews the world for another cyclical year.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, localized Bronze Age goddesses who hold ultimate state authority, to whom the earthly kings directly answer and provide massive tribute.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ancient grandmother goddess who provides the foundational elements of civilization, fertility, and animal husbandry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive seasonal festival designed to forcefully renew the fertility of the earth after the stagnation of the barren months.
GOLD SEAM: Teisheba shares direct iconographic and theological roots with the Hittite Tarhunna, both stemming from the broader Anatolian storm-god tradition.
GOLD SEAM: Both trace their lineage to the Hurrian Hebat, evolving into maternal anchors within heavily militarized Anatolian pantheons.

Cronus is the direct mythographic counterpart of Kumarbi in the Hurrian-Hittite succession cycle: both castrate the sky, swallow tokens of power, and are overthrown by their storm-god son — the middle generation the corpus's Kumarbi-to-Zeus seam was missing.
Anu, the primal Sky overthrown and unmanned in the Hurrian-Hittite 'Kingship in Heaven' cycle, is the accepted Near Eastern source-type for Ouranos's castration by Cronus in Hesiod.

Typhon (localised at Cilicia, on Anatolia's edge) is widely read as the Greek reflex of the Hittite/Hurrian Storm-God-vs-serpent Chaoskampf embodied in Illuyanka — the node the corpus's existing illuyanka-to-zeus seam pointed toward but lacked.
Kamrušepa performs the ritual that soothes Telipinu's wrath, the climactic pacification act that ends his withdrawal in the myth's resolution.
The Sun-God of Heaven and the Storm-God stand as the Hittite pantheon's two great male sovereigns, jointly invoked at the head of state prayers and treaty lists.

Two suns of the Hittite pantheon: Ištanu the celestial judge invoked first in personal prayer, Arinna the state's civic sun-goddess and royal patron — parallel solar sovereignties, one heavenly-masculine, one civic-feminine.
The 'calf of Teshub' — filial deity and personal protector of the late-Empire kings, famously carved embracing Tudḫaliya IV at Yazılıkaya alongside his father the Storm-God.
Via the Hurrian Hebat-Arinna equation, Šarruma is reckoned son of the Sun-Goddess as well as the Storm-God, completing the divine family triad carved at Yazılıkaya.

Underworld sovereigns of the same Ancient Near Eastern religious milieu — both rule the realm of the dead and are petitioned by royal courts on the king's behalf, in regions with documented cultural contact.
Inara recruits Ḫupašiya as her mortal ally to bind the drunken Illuyanka, then keeps him hidden as her consort in a mountain house — the bargain by which the serpent is captured.
Ḫupašiya physically binds the serpent with a rope after Inara's feast makes it drunk, the mortal hand that restrains the chaos-monster for the Storm-God's kill.
Ḫupašiya's binding of Illuyanka is the enabling act behind Tarhunna's victory, the mortal labor underlying the storm-god's recovery of his stolen heart and eyes.
The Storm-God of Hatti heads the divine witness-list every state treaty invokes.
The Sun-Goddess of Arinna stands alongside Tarhunna at the head of the treaty-witnessing 'thousand gods' formula.
The spring circuit's royal progress renews the cult of the Storm-God and the pantheon's chief shrines across roughly 38 days.
Two great spring renewal rites of the Hittite state cult attested side by side in the festival calendar — Purulli centered on the recited Illuyanka myth, AN.TAḪ.ŠUM on the royal circuit of shrines.
the same sky-god figure adopted from the Hurrian succession myth wholesale into Hittite state mythology, paralleling the file's existing Kumarbi/kumarbi and Ullikummi/ullikummi cross-file links.

The drought/chaos-serpent slain by a storm-god to free the land or waters — the Vedic and Hittite branches of the same Eurasian Chaoskampf template, reached independently of the direct Indra-Tarhunna cognate already drawn elsewhere in this shard.

The Hurrian-Hittite Kingship in Heaven cycle imports the Mesopotamian sky-king's name unchanged as Anu, then gives him a fall — castrated and dethroned by Kumarbi — that the Mesopotamian original never suffers.

dossier.essence: The state religion of the Hittite empire of Bronze Age Anatolia (c. 1650–1180 BCE), centered on the capital Hattusa. It famously embraced 'the thousand gods of Hatti' — a deliberately inclusive pantheon absorbing Hattian, Hurrian, Mesopotamian, and Luwian deities — headed by the Storm-God (Tarhunna, equated with Hurrian Teshub) and the Sun-Goddess of Arinna. It ended with the Bronze Age collapse around 1180 BCE.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is ruled by a divine kingship won and defended by violence: the 'Kingship in Heaven' cycle recounts successive sky-rulers — Alalu, Anu, Kumarbi, and finally the Storm-God — each overthrowing the last. Order is fragile and seasonal: gods can vanish in anger (Telipinu) so that fertility fails, and the serpent Illuyanka can defeat the Storm-God, who must recover his stolen heart and eyes. The dead descended to a shadowy underworld, but deceased kings were said to 'become gods' and received cult.
dossier.practice: A state cult led by the king (himself high priest), the queen, and a professional priesthood, organized around a dense festival calendar (notably the spring purulli and the AN.TAH.SUM festivals). Ritual centered on purification, substitution and scapegoat rites, oracular inquiry, and the appeasement of angry or vanished gods; many surviving texts are ritual 'recipes' attributed to named practitioners, often women (the 'Old Women'). Royal prayers and international treaties invoked the gods as witnesses and enforcers.
dossier.canon: No scripture — a cuneiform clay-tablet archive from Hattusa, catalogued by modern scholars as the Catalogue des Textes Hittites (CTH): the myths (Illuyanka, Telipinu, and the Kumarbi/Ullikummi cycle), festival and ritual texts, royal prayers, and treaties.
dossier.reflects: Religion as the maintenance of a contractually ordered but perpetually threatened cosmos — divine sovereignty as something earned, lost, and ritually recovered rather than eternally given.
sources: Harry A. Hoffner Jr., 'Hittite Myths' (2nd ed., 1998)
Billie Jean Collins, 'The Hittites and Their World' (2007)
Piotr Taracha, 'Religions of Second Millennium Anatolia' (2009)
